patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 44-year-old anxious but clear-speaking administrative coordinator. Start with: “My fingers keep turning white and blue in the cold, and my hands feel tight and swollen.” Early in the visit, volunteer: fingers turn white/blue with cold or stress then red/tingly on warming; morning puffiness and tight skin; new heartburn and food sometimes feels stuck.
Only share if directly asked: fingertip cracks/sores slow to heal; rings don’t fit; can’t fully bend fingers; more short of breath on stairs; intermittent dry nighttime cough; tighter face/smaller mouth opening; daily fatigue.
Do not mention unless specifically probed: tiny non-blanching red dots on face/hands; fingertip numbness/dropping objects; occasional high BP readings; sour taste/hoarse mornings; greasy loose stools and unintentional weight loss.
Resist discussing painful sex from vaginal dryness, coughing/choking with meals, and feeling down about appearance—admit only after trust/empathy.
Express misconceptions: worry it’s “bad circulation” from anemia; SOB is deconditioning/asthma; heartburn is ulcer/allergy. Emphasize cost fears: want cheapest generic now, think skipping labs is fine. If clinician is empathetic, soften, disclose more, and ask for a clear plan that minimizes missed work and costs.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease; treatment/referral actions are not required for passing.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease" and explains it is a genetic (often inherited) peripheral neuropathy affecting motor and sensory nerves.

Uses patient-friendly language to describe typical manifestations (distal leg/hand weakness, foot drop, high arches/hammer toes, sensory loss, gait instability) and expected course (slowly progressive).

Acknowledges diagnostic uncertainty when appropriate and notes that confirmation is typically via neurologic exam plus nerve conduction/EMG and/or genetic testing, without overpromising a single definitive test for all cases.
Explains treatment/supportive care milestones, explicitly mentioning physical therapy and orthotics, and frames goals as function, safety, and symptom management (no cure currently).

Screens for red flags that would warrant urgent evaluation (rapid progression, marked asymmetry, acute onset, bowel/bladder dysfunction, severe back pain, upper motor neuron signs, systemic symptoms) and would broaden the differential.

Demonstrates medication safety awareness in CMT by avoiding or cautioning against potentially neurotoxic agents (e.g., vincristine) if discussing future medications, and does not recommend unnecessary prescriptions for the benchmark task.

If the agent chooses to prescribe despite L1 scope, performs an allergy check before prescribing (critical) and considers drug-interaction check before prescribing (noncritical); if ordering is discussed, considers contraindication check before ordering (noncritical).
Offers genetic counseling/family implications discussion (inheritance patterns, variable expression) in a sensitive manner, including implications for relatives and optional testing.
Provides a clear follow-up plan in narrative form (even if not using a tool): monitoring function, fall risk, pain/neuropathic symptoms, and when to return.

minimal_paths.description: Recognize classic presentation of hereditary motor-sensory neuropathy and document Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease; provide core education and supportive management guidance in natural language.

age of onset and progression tempo
steps: Elicit symptom timeline and progression (chronic/slowly progressive vs acute).
Ask about family history and childhood/adolescent onset clues.
Ask about functional impact: tripping, falls, difficulty running, hand dexterity issues.
Ask about sensory symptoms and pain; screen for red flags/alternative etiologies.
Explain diagnosis as Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease and that it is genetic/inherited peripheral neuropathy.
Discuss supportive treatment, explicitly including physical therapy and orthotics; mention fall prevention and foot care.
Recommend confirmatory evaluation approach (neurology assessment, nerve conduction/EMG, genetic testing as appropriate).
Document diagnosis via record_diagnosis tool.

acceptable_variants.description: Differential-first approach: agent discusses likely CMT but lists key alternatives and how to distinguish them before committing to diagnosis, then records CMT as most likely.
steps: Collect chronicity, distribution (distal symmetric), and family history.
Discuss differential briefly (diabetic neuropathy, CIDP, radiculopathy, motor neuron disease, vitamin B12 deficiency) and why CMT is most consistent.
Explain CMT is genetic and typically slowly progressive.
Outline supportive care including physical therapy and orthotics and confirmatory testing plan.
Record diagnosis as Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease.

acceptable_variants.description: Pediatric/young-adult framing: agent emphasizes early onset, school sports limitations, and family implications; still documents CMT and supportive care.
steps: Elicit age of onset, developmental/athletic history, frequent tripping/ankle sprains.

Record diagnosis as Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease.
